title: Add the ^ power operator to the Pratt-parser calculator
description:
The calc crate is a three-file Pratt-parser calculator (src/lexer.rs, src/parser.rs, src/eval.rs) supporting + - * / and unary minus. Your task is to add the ^ (caret) exponentiation operator.

Operator semantics — these equalities define the required behaviour:
- eval_str("2^3^2") == 512  (^ is right-associative: 2^(3^2) = 2^9 = 512)
- eval_str("-2^2") == -4   (unary minus applies to the whole power: -(2^2))
- eval_str("2^-1") == 0.5  (unary minus is permitted in the exponent)
- eval_str("2*3^2") == 18  (^ binds tighter than * and /: 2*(3^2) = 18)

Two prose statements that must also hold:
- ^ is right-associative
- ^ binds tighter than * and /

The committed state is RED on exactly one test (`power_basic`, which asserts eval_str("2^3") == Ok(8.0)) because the lexer does not yet recognise the `^` character — it returns Err on unknown tokens, so no compile error, just a runtime test failure.
acceptance_criteria: src/lexer.rs: add a Caret token variant to Token and handle the `^` character in tokenize (currently the `^` character falls through to the unknown-token Err branch)

src/parser.rs: add a Pow(Box<Expr>, Box<Expr>) variant to Expr; add a right-associative binding-power entry for Caret (left_bp > right_bp so the right-hand recursive call accepts the same level again); add the Pow arm in parse_expr
src/eval.rs: add a Pow(base, exp) arm that evaluates to eval(base)?.powf(eval(exp)?)
eval_str("-2^2") == -4  (unary minus applies after the power, not before)
eval_str("2^-1") == 0.5  (unary minus is valid in the exponent position)

files_to_modify.change: Add a Caret variant to the Token enum and handle the `^` character in the tokenize match (currently `^` hits the catch-all unknown-token Err branch).

Add a Pow(Box<Expr>, Box<Expr>) variant to the Expr enum; add a right-associative binding-power pair for the Caret token in infix_bp where left_bp > right_bp (e.g. (7, 6)) placing ^ above * and /; add the Pow arm in the infix match inside parse_expr.
Add an arm for Expr::Pow(base, exp) that returns Ok(eval(base)?.powf(eval(exp)?)).
